title: Periodic Table Basics
description: Test your knowledge of element symbols, periods, and groups.

questions.question: What is the chemical symbol for Gold?
questions.explanation: Gold's symbol Au comes from its Latin name Aurum.

questions.question: Which element has the atomic number 1?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen (H) is the lightest and most abundant element in the universe with atomic number 1.

questions.question: What is the symbol for Sodium?
questions.explanation: Sodium's symbol Na comes from its Latin name Natrium.

questions.question: Which element is represented by the symbol Fe?
questions.explanation: Fe stands for Ferrum, the Latin name for Iron.

questions.question: How many elements are in Period 2 of the periodic table?
questions.explanation: Period 2 contains 8 elements: Li, Be, B, C, N, O, F, and Ne.

title: Chemical Bonding
description: Explore ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.

questions.question: What type of bond forms between a metal and a non-metal?
questions.explanation: Ionic bonds form when electrons are transferred from a metal to a non-metal, creating oppositely charged ions.

questions.question: In a covalent bond, atoms share what?
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds involve the sharing of electron pairs between atoms.

questions.question: Which molecule contains a triple bond?
questions.explanation: Nitrogen gas (N₂) has a triple bond — one sigma and two pi bonds — making it very stable.

questions.question: What is the shape of a water molecule?
questions.explanation: Water has a bent shape due to the two lone pairs on oxygen repelling the bonding pairs.

questions.question: Which type of bond is the strongest?
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds, especially double and triple bonds, are generally the strongest type of chemical bond.

title: Acids & Bases
description: pH scale, Arrhenius, and Brønsted–Lowry theory.

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution at 25°C?
questions.explanation: Pure water at 25°C has equal concentrations of H⁺ and OH⁻ ions, giving a pH of exactly 7.

questions.question: According to Brønsted–Lowry theory, an acid is a substance that:
questions.explanation: Brønsted–Lowry acids are proton (H⁺) donors, while bases are proton acceptors.

questions.question: Which of these is a strong acid?
questions.explanation: HCl completely dissociates in water, making it a strong acid. The others are weak acids.

questions.question: What is the conjugate base of H₂SO₄?
questions.explanation: H₂SO₄ donates one proton to form HSO₄⁻, its conjugate base.

questions.question: A buffer solution resists changes in pH because it contains:
options: A strong acid and its salt
A weak acid and its conjugate base
questions.answer: A weak acid and its conjugate base
questions.explanation: Buffers work because the weak acid neutralises added base, while the conjugate base neutralises added acid.

questions.question: What is the electron configuration of Carbon (Z=6)?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 electrons: 2 fill 1s, 2 fill 2s, and 2 occupy the 2p subshell.

questions.question: The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill orbitals:
options: Starting from the highest energy level
Starting from the lowest energy level
Two at a time in every orbital
questions.answer: Starting from the lowest energy level
questions.explanation: Aufbau (German: 'building up') means electrons occupy the lowest available energy orbital first.

questions.question: How many electrons can a 3d subshell hold?
questions.explanation: The d subshell has 5 orbitals, and each orbital holds 2 electrons, giving a maximum of 10.

questions.question: Which element is an exception to the Aufbau principle due to extra stability of a half-filled d subshell?
questions.explanation: Chromium has [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ instead of [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s² because a half-filled d subshell has extra stability.

questions.question: Hund's rule states that electrons in degenerate orbitals will:
options: Occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins first
Fill the highest energy orbital first
questions.answer: Occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins first
questions.explanation: Hund's rule minimises electron repulsion by spreading electrons across degenerate orbitals before pairing.

title: Chemical Thermodynamics
description: Enthalpy, entropy, Gibbs free energy, and spontaneity.

questions.question: A reaction has ΔH = −120 kJ/mol and ΔS = −200 J/(mol·K). At what temperature does it become non-spontaneous?
questions.explanation: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS. Setting ΔG = 0: T = ΔH/ΔS = −120,000 / −200 = 600 K. Above 600 K, ΔG > 0.

questions.question: Which statement about entropy is correct?
options: Entropy decreases in all spontaneous processes
The entropy of a perfect crystal at 0 K is zero
Entropy is always negative for exothermic reactions
Entropy cannot be calculated for gases
questions.answer: The entropy of a perfect crystal at 0 K is zero
questions.explanation: This is the Third Law of Thermodynamics: a perfect crystalline substance at absolute zero has zero entropy.

questions.question: The standard enthalpy of formation (ΔHf°) of an element in its standard state is:
questions.explanation: By definition, the standard enthalpy of formation of any element in its reference state is zero.

questions.question: For the reaction N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) → 2NH₃(g), what is the sign of ΔS?
options: Positive, more moles of gas formed
Negative, fewer moles of gas formed
questions.answer: Negative, fewer moles of gas formed
questions.explanation: 4 moles of gas react to give 2 moles, reducing molecular disorder. ΔS is negative.

questions.question: Hess's Law is a consequence of which thermodynamic principle?
questions.explanation: Because enthalpy is a state function, the total enthalpy change is independent of the path taken.

questions.question: Which functional group is characteristic of an aldehyde?
questions.explanation: Aldehydes contain a –CHO (formyl) group where the carbonyl carbon is bonded to at least one hydrogen.

questions.question: What type of reaction converts an alkene to a haloalkane?
questions.explanation: The π bond of an alkene acts as a nucleophile, attacking an electrophilic halogen molecule via addition.

questions.question: In Markovnikov's rule, when HX adds to an unsymmetrical alkene, the H adds to:
options: The carbon with fewer hydrogen atoms
The carbon with more hydrogen atoms
The carbon bonded to the halogen
questions.answer: The carbon with more hydrogen atoms
questions.explanation: Markovnikov's rule: H adds to the carbon already bearing more H atoms, forming the more stable carbocation intermediate.

questions.question: Which reagent is used for the oxidation of a primary alcohol to a carboxylic acid?
questions.explanation: Acidified dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄) is a strong oxidant that oxidises primary alcohols fully to carboxylic acids.

questions.question: What is the IUPAC name of CH₃CH₂CH(CH₃)CH₂OH?
questions.explanation: The longest chain containing –OH has 4 carbons (butanol). The methyl branch is on C2. Hydroxyl is on C1: 2-methylbutan-1-ol.

title: Periodic Table Basics
description: Test your knowledge of element symbols, periods, and groups.

questions.question: Which element is a noble gas?
questions.explanation: Neon (Ne) is a noble gas with a full valence shell, making it chemically inert.

questions.question: Which element is in Group 1 of the periodic table?
questions.explanation: Group 1 elements are alkali metals; Sodium (Na) is one of them.

questions.question: Which element has the highest electronegativity?
questions.explanation: Fluorine is the most electronegative element on the periodic table.

questions.question: Which period contains the element Magnesium?
questions.explanation: Magnesium (Mg) is in Period 3 of the periodic table.

questions.question: What type of element is Bromine?
questions.explanation: Bromine (Br) is a halogen, located in Group 17.

title: Chemical Bonding
description: Explore ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.

questions.question: Which bond forms between two non-metals?
questions.explanation: Non-metal atoms share electrons to form covalent bonds.

questions.question: Which molecule exhibits hydrogen bonding?
questions.explanation: Water molecules form hydrogen bonds due to polar O–H bonds.

questions.question: Metallic bonding is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Metallic bonds involve a lattice of positive ions with delocalized electrons.

questions.question: Which bond is strongest?
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds involve shared electron pairs, making them stronger than hydrogen or Van der Waals interactions.

questions.question: Which compound is ionic?
questions.explanation: Sodium chloride forms ions (Na⁺ and Cl⁻), creating an ionic lattice.

title: Acids & Bases
description: pH scale, Arrhenius, and Brønsted–Lowry theory.

questions.question: Which acid is found in vinegar?
questions.explanation: Acetic acid (CH₃COOH) is the main component of vinegar.

questions.question: Which base is used in soap making?
questions.explanation: Sodium hydroxide reacts with fats to produce soap via saponification.

questions.question: A solution with pH 10 is:
questions.explanation: pH > 7 indicates a basic solution.

questions.question: What is the conjugate acid of NH₃?
questions.explanation: NH₃ accepts a proton to form NH₄⁺, its conjugate acid.

questions.question: Which of these is a weak acid?
questions.explanation: Acetic acid only partially dissociates in water, making it a weak acid.

questions.question: Which element has configuration [Ne] 3s² 3p³?
questions.explanation: Phosphorus (P) has 15 electrons and fills 3s² 3p³ after Neon.

questions.question: Which subshell can hold a maximum of 14 electrons?
questions.explanation: f-subshell has 7 orbitals, each holding 2 electrons, for a total of 14.

questions.question: Which principle states no two electrons can have identical quantum numbers?
questions.explanation: The Pauli Exclusion Principle restricts electrons to unique quantum numbers.

questions.question: Which element is a transition metal?
questions.explanation: Iron (Fe) is a d-block transition metal.

questions.question: According to Hund's Rule, electrons occupy degenerate orbitals by:
questions.explanation: Electrons spread across degenerate orbitals with parallel spins before pairing.

title: Chemical Thermodynamics
description: Enthalpy, entropy, Gibbs free energy, and spontaneity.

questions.question: An endothermic reaction has ΔH:
questions.explanation: Endothermic reactions absorb heat, so ΔH > 0.

questions.question: At equilibrium, Gibbs free energy change ΔG is:
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, ΔG = 0.

questions.question: Which process releases heat?
questions.explanation: Condensation releases latent heat to the surroundings.

questions.question: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS applies to:
questions.explanation: Gibbs free energy equation applies to all chemical reactions.

questions.question: Hess's Law depends on:
questions.explanation: Hess's Law works because enthalpy is a state function.

questions.question: Which functional group is a ketone?
questions.explanation: Ketones have a carbonyl group bonded to two carbons.

questions.question: Which reaction converts an alcohol to an alkene?
questions.explanation: Alcohol loses H₂O to form an alkene in a dehydration reaction.

questions.question: Which compound is aromatic?
questions.explanation: Benzene has a conjugated π-electron system and follows Hückel's rule.

questions.question: Primary alcohol has –OH attached to:
questions.explanation: Primary alcohols have –OH on a carbon connected to only one other carbon.

questions.question: Esters contain which functional group?
questions.explanation: Esters have –COO– linking carbonyl and oxygen atoms.
